San Mateo, California  

Hillsdale Mall
Life, Death & Rebirth

The story of the Peninsula's gathering spot — from dairy farmland to shopping destination to urban village.

Founded1954
Founded ByDavid D. Bohannon
LocationSan Mateo, California

Primary Sources

The Interviews

David D. Bohannon II
President, Bohannon Organization

Grandson of the founder. Oversees the continued stewardship of the Hillsdale property and the Bohannon Development Company's broader portfolio across the Peninsula.

Mitch Postel
President, San Mateo Historical Association

Historian and archivist with deep expertise in San Mateo County's development history, from the postwar suburban boom through the present era of mixed-use redevelopment.

Overview

Three Defining Eras

1
1950s – 1970s

Post-war Suburban Optimism

Initially dairy farmland, Hillsdale rises to become the Peninsula's premier open-air shopping destination, anchored by Macy's, Sears, and H. Liebes.

2
1980s – 1990s

Golden Age of the Indoor Mall

A renovation encloses the mall, brings in Nordstrom and Mervyn's, and cements Hillsdale as the dominant retail center on the Peninsula.

3
2000s – Present

Walkable, Mixed-Use Hub

Facing disruption, Hillsdale reinvents itself as a recreational experience, as opposed to a pure shopping destination.

Era 1 · Origins

The Developer: David D. Bohannon

David Bohannon was born in San Francisco in 1898. He began a successful homebuilding career during the Great Depression, and pivoted to shopping malls when he understood that they would transform the nature of retail.

  • Built over 25,000 homes on the Peninsula in the 1930s–40s
  • Acquired 848 acres from the Burleigh H. Murray Ranch in 1939 — formerly dairy farmland
  • Modeled Hillsdale after Country Club Plaza in Kansas City (1922) and Westlake Shopping Center in California (1951)
  • Motivated equally by wealth creation and civic duty
1939
Year Bohannon acquired the Hillsdale land — 15 years before the mall opened
1942
Andrew Williams Grocery opens — the first structure built on the Hillsdale site

San Mateo at the Time of Construction

San Mateo during the early 1950s

Construction of Hillsdale Shopping Center, 1953–1954.

CONTEXT

San Mateo Population Growth, 1930–2024

▲ Population nearly tripled 1940–1950, then doubled again by 1960 — creating a vast consumer base for Hillsdale's early success.

Era 1 · 1954–1979

Successful Launch & Early Years

When Hillsdale opened in December 1954, it met with immediate success. The mall transformed shopping into a leisure activity and provided for the growing car-centric nature of the Peninsula with vast parking space.

Original Anchors

Macy's Sears H. Liebes Emporium (1962)
1941
Andrew Williams Grocery opens as the first store at Hillsdale, later rebranded as Mayfair Market
1951
Sears opens as the first anchor tenant
1954
Grand opening with 53 stores — the first suburban Macy's in California
1954
H. Liebes added as third anchor — a high-end fashion outlet
1962
Emporium opens as the fourth anchor department store
1979
Hillsdale adds enclosure and second floor to compete with Fashion Island

Early Dining Options

Hillsdale restaurants in 1950s and 1960s

Photos from the Early Years

Contributions to Civic Life

  Civil Defense Stockpile

Hillsdale's underground tunnels held 50-gallon drums of water, graham crackers, honey, and sand as a federally designated Cold War fallout shelter. The same tunnels also stored Bufano's sculptures.

  Bufano Sculpture Program

Bohannon commissioned works by world-famous sculptor Beniamino Bufano in the 1950s. In 1960, an actual Bufano studio was established on the Hillsdale property where he worked and taught students.

  Teen Cruising Culture

Hillsdale marked the northern end of the El Camino Real cruise route, especially popular with Peninsula teenagers. The parking lot was a key gathering spot during this era.

  Community Events

Hillsdale hosted school performances, holiday exhibitions, and Christmas celebrations. Actress Shirley Temple and CA governer candidate Ronald Reagan both visited in the mall's early days.

Civil defense water drums

Underground tunnels

Bufano sculptures

ERA 2 · 1980S–1990S

The Golden Era

Facing competition from Fashion Island — a new enclosed mall two miles north — the Bohannon family decided to renovate. The 1982 renovation transformed Hillsdale into a modern two-level enclosed mall.

New Anchors

Nordstrom (NorCal's first location) and Mervyn's joined Macy's, Emporium, Sears, and H. Liebes — creating six anchors total.

Outcompeting Fashion Island

Fashion Island's poor location led to lackluster consumer spending and eventual bankruptcy, while Hillsdale thrived.

Drivers of the Boom

Tech wealth from Cisco, Oracle, and Apple, combined with population growth and "mall culture," created an ideal consumer landscape for Hillsdale.

Peak Profitability

By the early 1990s, Hillsdale was one of the top-performing malls per square foot in Northern California.

Photos from the Golden Era

Hillsdale Mall shortly after the 1982 enclosure renovation.

Era 3 · 2000s–2010s

Modern Decline

Hillsdale's challenges were far from unique. Across America, enclosed malls faced an existential crisis as consumer behavior shifted and digital commerce took hold.

Big Box & Online

Rise of warehouse stores and e-commerce fundamentally undercut the enclosed mall's appeal and value proposition.

Margin Pressure

Declining sales per sq ft made leases untenable. Department stores were forced to cut staff and inventory.

Stanford Rivalry

Stanford Shopping Center attracted the high-spending younger shoppers, and soon began consistently outperforming Hillsdale.

Store Closures

Mervyn's closed in 2008 and Sears closed in 2016. The bankruptcy of two anchors illustrated retail's broader financial struggles in the digital era.

Era 3 · Rebirth

Reinvention: Two Renovation Phases

1
First Renovation
Completed 2019

A $290M+ transformation of the old Sears site into the North Block — an outdoor experiential district.

  • Cinepolis cinema — first in Northern California
  • Pinstripes — first in Northern California
  • Apple, Lululemon, expanded food court
  • Outdoor dining terrace opened Nov 17, 2018
  • Goal: retail, dining, and recreational experience
2
Second Renovation
Planning Phase

A more ambitious vision: transform Hillsdale from a shopping center into a walkable mixed-use village.

  • Mixed-use residential + retail "urban village"
  • Addresses San Mateo's housing shortage
  • Retaining outdoor retail component
  • Transforming a place to shop into a place to live

Renovation Photos

North Block, 2020

Second renovation model

Reflections

01
Hillsdale has been a valuable addition to the Peninsula since its founding — not merely as a place to shop, but as a genuine community institution serving civic, cultural, and social functions for over 70 years.
02
The mall has mirrored the economic and social trends of the community, the state, and the country — from postwar optimism and population growth, to Cold War anxiety, to mall culture, to e-commerce's disruptive impact.
03
Hillsdale has endured competition, renovation, and the death of the American mall. Through it all, it has proven to be a durable communal hub of the San Francisco Peninsula.
Hillsdale Mall — San Mateo, CA
 ·
